messages.role: u ed subjects of regulation under section 1 of the Open-Ended Investment Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 2002?

messages.content: Section 1(2) indicates that regulations may address (a) the incorporation in Northern Ireland of bodies corporate and their registration by the Authority; (d) the management and operation of such bodies and their property; and (e) the powers, duties, rights and liabilities of such bodies and of other persons, including directors or sole director, depositary, shareholders (and persons holding the beneficial title without the legal title), auditors, and any persons acting or purporting to act on the body's behalf. The Act therefore regulates open-ended investment companies and the wider constellation of persons involved in their incorporation, management and operation.
When did the Open-Ended Investment Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 2002 come into operation, and what is its current status?

Section 4 provides that the preceding provisions came into operation on such day as the Department of Enterprise, Trade and Investment might by order appoint; that commencement power was exercised by SR 2004/333. The Act has since been repealed with effect from 1 October 2009 by the Companies Act 2006 (sections 1286(2)(c), 1295 and 1300(2), and Schedule 16), subject to transitional provisions and savings (S.I. 2008/2860 and S.I. 2011/1265).

Under the Open-Ended Investment Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 2002, what happens if the Financial Services Authority considers that a director or depositary of an open-ended investment company should be removed?

Section 1(2)(j) permits regulations under section 1 to enable the Authority to apply to a court for an order removing and replacing any director or depositary of such a body. The statute itself does not effect removal directly; it authorises subordinate legislation under which the Authority may seek such a court order, and section 1(2)(i) also permits regulations requiring such a body (or any director or depositary of it) to comply with directions given by the Authority.

What minimum capital requirement does the Open-Ended Investment Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 2002 impose on open-ended investment companies, and what is the tax treatment of distributions under the Act?

The Act does not address minimum capital requirements and does not prescribe any tax treatment of distributions. It is an enabling statute: section 1 authorises the Department to make regulations covering matters such as incorporation, constitution, management, mergers, winding up and investigations, and section 1(2)(l) allows regulations corresponding to Chapter III of Part XVII of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000, but the Act itself fixes no capital minima or tax rules. I cannot supply those details from this Act.
What does section 1 of the Company and Business Names (Chamber of Commerce, Etc.) Act 1999 require the Secretary of State to do?

Section 1 provides that it is the duty of the Secretary of State to secure that the expression "chamber of commerce" and its Welsh equivalent ("siambr fasnach") is specified (a) in regulations under section 55 of the Companies Act 2006 (company names requiring approval of the Secretary of State) and (b) in regulations under section 1194 of that Act (business names requiring approval of the Secretary of State), as an expression the registration or use of which as or as part of a name requires the Secretary of State's approval.

Under the Company and Business Names (Chamber of Commerce, Etc.) Act 1999, must the Secretary of State consult a representative body before approving a company name that includes the phrase "chambers of industry"?

Section 2(1) requires the Secretary of State, before determining under section 55 of the Companies Act 2006 whether to approve the registration, to consult at least one relevant representative body where the name includes (a) "chamber of commerce"/"siambr fasnach" or (b) any other expression for the time being specified in regulations under that section which begins with the words "chamber of" or "chambers of" (or the Welsh equivalents). So the consultation duty applies to "chambers of industry" only if that phrase has been specified in the relevant regulations; if not specified, section 2 does not require consultation about it.
Does the Company and Business Names (Chamber of Commerce, Etc.) Act 1999 extend to Northern Ireland?
Section 5(3) expressly provides that the Act does not extend to Northern Ireland.
Who are the "relevant representative bodies" for the purposes of the Company and Business Names (Chamber of Commerce, Etc.) Act 1999?

Section 4(1) defines the relevant representative bodies as (a) the British Chambers of Commerce and (b) the body known as the Scottish Chambers of Commerce. Section 4(2) permits the Secretary of State by order to amend subsection (1) by adding or deleting the name of any body (whether corporate or unincorporated), and section 4(3) provides that such an order is made by statutory instrument subject to annulment in pursuance of a resolution of either House of Parliament.
To what kinds of names does section 3 of the Company and Business Names (Chamber of Commerce, Etc.) Act 1999 apply?

Section 3(1) applies the consultation duty to business names (approval under section 1194 of the Companies Act 2006) that include (a) "chamber of commerce" or "siambr fasnach", or (b) any other expression specified in regulations under section 1194 which begins with the words "chamber of" or "chambers of" (or the Welsh equivalents). Section 3 mirrors section 2 but for business names rather than company names, and section 3(2) permits the Secretary of State to publish guidance on factors that may be taken into account in approval decisions.
When did the Company and Business Names (Chamber of Commerce, Etc.) Act 1999 come into force?

The Act received Royal Assent on 27 July 1999. Section 5(2) provides that it comes into force on such day as the Secretary of State may by order made by statutory instrument appoint. That power was fully exercised with a commencement date of 10 May 2001 appointed by S.I. 2001/258, article 2.

Under the Company and Business Names (Chamber of Commerce, Etc.) Act 1999, what must happen before the Secretary of State decides whether to approve the registration of a new company under a name such as "East Midlands Chamber of Commerce Ltd"?

Section 2(1) requires the Secretary of State, before determining under section 55 of the Companies Act 2006 whether to approve the registration, to consult at least one relevant representative body (defined in section 4 as the British Chambers of Commerce or the Scottish Chambers of Commerce). Under section 2(2) the Secretary of State may also publish guidance on factors that may be taken into account in deciding whether to approve a name to which section 2 applies.

What criminal penalty does the Company and Business Names (Chamber of Commerce, Etc.) Act 1999 impose on a person who uses the words "chamber of commerce" in a business name without approval, and what is the appeal route against a refusal to approve?

The Act does not address criminal penalties and does not create or describe any appeal route against a refusal to approve. Its operative provisions (sections 1–3) simply require the Secretary of State to specify "chamber of commerce" (and its Welsh equivalent) as a protected expression and to consult a relevant representative body before deciding whether to approve a company or business name. Any enforcement, penalty or appeal mechanism would have to be found in the Companies Act 2006 (whose sections 55 and 1194 are cross-referred to), not in this Act. I cannot supply those details from this Act.
What does section 1 of the Insurance Companies (Reserves) Act 1995 do?

Section 1(1) inserts a new section 34A ("General business: equalisation reserve") into the Insurance Companies Act 1982, after section 34. The inserted section 34A(1) requires every insurance company to which the section applies, which carries on general business of a prescribed description, to maintain an "equalisation reserve" in respect of that business in accordance with regulations. Section 1(2) also amends section 32 (margin of solvency) of the 1982 Act by inserting a new subsection (7) providing that, in applying section 32(5), a company's liabilities are treated as increased by the amount of any reserve maintained under new section 34A.
Does the Insurance Companies (Reserves) Act 1995 itself prescribe the categories of general business in respect of which an equalisation reserve must be maintained?

No. Section 1 of the Act inserts section 34A into the Insurance Companies Act 1982, and the inserted section 34A(1) leaves the description of general business to which the reserve requirement applies to be prescribed by regulations. The 1995 Act sets up the framework; the detail — including which businesses are covered, when amounts are placed in or taken from the reserve, and how they are calculated — is left to subordinate legislation under section 34A(4).
Which insurance companies are within the equalisation reserve requirement introduced by the Insurance Companies (Reserves) Act 1995, and which may be excluded?

The inserted section 34A(2) (via section 1 of the 1995 Act) applies the reserve requirement to insurance companies to which Part II of the Insurance Companies Act 1982 applies that (a) have their head office in the United Kingdom, (b) have UK business restricted to reinsurance, or (c) have a head office not in a member State. Section 34A(3) further provides that the section does not apply to insurance companies of a description prescribed for the purposes of that subsection — so the regulations may carve out specific categories. Section 2 of the 1995 Act amends section 68 of the 1982 Act (power to modify Part II in relation to particular companies) so that the Secretary of State's modification power can extend to section 34A and regulations made under it.
How does the Insurance Companies (Reserves) Act 1995 describe an "equalisation reserve"?

The new section 34A(1) of the Insurance Companies Act 1982 — as inserted by section 1 of the 1995 Act — describes an "equalisation reserve" as a reserve that certain insurance companies must maintain, in accordance with regulations made for the purposes of that section, in respect of general business of a prescribed description. Section 34A(4) empowers regulations to specify the circumstances in which, and times at which, amounts are to be placed to or taken from the reserve, how those amounts are to be determined, and other incidental matters.
Does the Insurance Companies (Reserves) Act 1995 extend to Northern Ireland?

Section 4(3) expressly provides that the Act extends to Northern Ireland. Section 3 accordingly makes consequential amendments to Schedule 9A to the Companies (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 as well as to Schedule 9A to the Companies Act 1985 (form and content of accounts of insurance companies and groups).
When did the operative provisions of the Insurance Companies (Reserves) Act 1995 come into force, and what is the Act's current status?

Section 4(2) provides that sections 1 and 3 come into force on such day as the Secretary of State may by order made by statutory instrument appoint. That power was exercised on 24 March 1996 by S.I. 1996/945 (article 2), which appointed 30 April 1996 as the commencement day. The Act has since been repealed with effect from 1 December 2001 by S.I. 2001/3649 (articles 1 and 3(e)).
Under the Insurance Companies (Reserves) Act 1995, what happens to a company's reported solvency margin when it maintains an equalisation reserve?

Section 1(2) amends section 32 of the Insurance Companies Act 1982 by inserting a new subsection (7) providing that, in applying section 32(5) (margin of solvency), the amount of the company's liabilities is taken to be increased by the amount of any reserve maintained under the newly inserted section 34A. The practical effect is that equalisation reserves count against the available margin of solvency.

What criminal offence does the Insurance Companies (Reserves) Act 1995 create for an insurance company that fails to maintain the required equalisation reserve, and what is the minimum percentage of premiums that must be transferred to the reserve each year?

The Act does not create any such criminal offence and does not fix any minimum percentage of premiums to be transferred. It is an amending statute: section 1 inserts the reserve requirement into the Insurance Companies Act 1982 (as section 34A) and leaves the determination of amounts and the circumstances of transfer to regulations made under section 34A(4). Any offences, percentage figures or enforcement machinery would have to come from that secondary legislation or from the underlying 1982 Act, not from this Act. I cannot supply those details from this Act.
What does section 2(2) of the Companies (Beneficial Interests) Act 1983 define as a "residual interest"?

Section 2(2) provides that a "residual interest" means a right of the company or subsidiary in question ("the residual beneficiary") to receive any of the trust property in the event of (a) all the liabilities arising under the scheme having been satisfied or provided for; (b) the residual beneficiary ceasing to participate in the scheme; or (c) the trust property at any time exceeding what is necessary for satisfying the liabilities arising or expected to arise under the scheme.
Under the Companies (Beneficial Interests) Act 1983, is a right to receive trust property disregarded even where it depends on the exercise of a discretion by the trustee?

Yes. Section 2(3) provides that, in subsection (2), references to a right include references to a right dependent on the exercise of a discretion vested by the scheme in the trustee or any other person, and references to liabilities arising under a scheme include references to liabilities that have resulted or may result from the exercise of any such discretion.
What limitation does the Companies (Beneficial Interests) Act 1983 place on the disregard of employer charges in the case of employees' share schemes, compared with pension schemes?

Section 3(1) provides that where shares are held on trust for a pension scheme, both categories of right listed in subsection (2) are disregarded: (a) charges, liens or set-offs against a member's benefit to enable the employer to recover a monetary obligation from the member, and (b) rights to amounts recoverable or retainable under section 47 of the Social Security Pensions Act 1975 or otherwise as reimbursement for state scheme premiums. But for an employees' share scheme, section 3(1)(b) disregards only the paragraph (a) rights; the paragraph (b) state-pension-premium rights are not disregarded.
How does the Companies (Beneficial Interests) Act 1983 define "pension scheme" and "relevant benefits"?

Section 5(2) defines "pension scheme" as any scheme for the provision of benefits consisting of or including relevant benefits for or in respect of employees or former employees. "Relevant benefits" means any pension, lump sum, gratuity or other like benefit given or to be given on retirement or on death or in anticipation of retirement, or in connection with past service, after retirement or death. Section 5(3) treats a director of a company as employed by it for the purposes of section 3(2)(a) and section 5(2); section 5(4) adopts the definition of "employees' share scheme" from section 87(1) of the Companies Act 1980.
To which earlier companies legislation does the Companies (Beneficial Interests) Act 1983 apply, and does it extend to Northern Ireland?

Section 1 lists the provisions of the Companies Act 1948 and the Companies Act 1980 to which the Act applies: (a) section 27(2) of the 1948 Act; (b) paragraph 60(2) of Schedule 8 and paragraph 15(3) of Schedule 8A to the 1948 Act; (c) section 36(6)(a) of the 1980 Act; and (d) section 37(1)(b), (c) and (d) of the 1980 Act. Section 7(3) provides that, except for section 6 (which enables corresponding provision to be made for Northern Ireland by Order in Council), the Act does not extend to Northern Ireland.
When was the Companies (Beneficial Interests) Act 1983 passed, and what is the temporal effect of its provisions?

The Act was passed on 26 July 1983. Section 1 provides that the Act has effect, and is deemed always to have had effect, for the interpretation of the listed provisions of the Companies Act 1948 and the Companies Act 1980. Paragraph 4 of the Schedule applies to residual interests that vest in possession whether the date of vesting is before or after the passing of the Act, treating pre-passing vestings as if they had occurred on the date the Act was passed.
Under the Companies (Beneficial Interests) Act 1983, what happens if a residual interest vests in possession so that a subsidiary loses its exemption from section 27 of the Companies Act 1948?

Paragraph 1 of the Schedule (given effect by section 2(4)) provides that section 27 of the 1948 Act shall not prevent the subsidiary from continuing to be a member of its holding company, but — subject to section 27(2) of that Act — the subsidiary shall have no right from the date of vesting to vote at meetings of the holding company or of any class of its members.

What penalty does the Companies (Beneficial Interests) Act 1983 impose on a company that fails to disregard a residual interest it is required to disregard, and what filing obligations does the Act create with the Registrar of Companies?

The Act does not impose any penalty and does not create any filing obligations with the Registrar of Companies. It is an interpretive statute: section 1 directs how certain references in the Companies Acts 1948 and 1980 are to be read, sections 2 to 4 specify the interests and rights to be disregarded, and the Schedule deals with the consequences when a residual interest vests in possession. Any penalties or filing duties derive from the underlying 1948 and 1980 Acts, not from this Act. I cannot supply those details from this Act.
What does section 1(1) of the Statutory Companies (Redeemable Stock) Act 1915 permit a statutory company to do?

Section 1(1) provides that where a statutory company (as defined by the Act) is authorised to raise any preference or debenture stock, it may create and issue that stock so as to be redeemable on such terms and conditions as may be specified in a resolution of the company passed at a special meeting convened for the purpose.
Under the Statutory Companies (Redeemable Stock) Act 1915, can a statutory company call in and pay off its redeemable preference or debenture stock before the fixed date of redemption?

Yes, if the relevant resolution so provides. Section 1(2)(a) states that, if it is so provided in the resolution, the statutory company may call in and pay off the stock or any part of it at any time before the fixed date of redemption. Section 1(2)(b) also permits the company, on the same condition, to redeem the stock by issuing substituted stock to any stockholder with that stockholder's consent.
What temporal and consent limitations does the Statutory Companies (Redeemable Stock) Act 1915 place on the creation or issue of redeemable stock under its powers?

Section 1(5) limits the Act's operation to stock authorised to be created or issued before the outbreak of the present war, or after the outbreak of the war and before the commencement of the Act. It further provides that redeemable stock shall not be created or issued in pursuance of the powers given by the Act during the continuance of the present war and a period of twelve months thereafter, except with the consent of the Treasury.
How does the Statutory Companies (Redeemable Stock) Act 1915 define "statutory company" and "special Act"?

Section 2(1) defines "statutory company" as any railway company, canal company, dock company, water company, or other company incorporated by special Act, which is for the time being authorised under such an Act to construct, work, own, or carry on any railway, canal, dock, water, or other public undertaking, and includes any person or body of persons so authorised. The same subsection provides that "special Act" includes any certificate or order having the force of an Act.
Do the powers in the Statutory Companies (Redeemable Stock) Act 1915 apply to preference shares and debentures as well as to stock, and do they exclude other statutory powers?

Section 2(2) provides that the provisions of the Act apply to preference shares in the same manner as they apply to preference stock, and to debentures in the same manner as they apply to debenture stock. Section 2(3) adds that the powers given by the Act are in addition to, and not in derogation of, any other powers under any other Act, and may be exercised in the same manner as if the Act had not passed.
When did the Statutory Companies (Redeemable Stock) Act 1915 receive Royal Assent, and what is the temporal effect of section 1(4)?

Section 3 (the short title) and the enacting words record that the Act was passed on 19 May 1915. Section 1(4) gives retrospective effect to pre-Act resolutions: a resolution of a statutory company passed before the commencement of the Act and after the outbreak of the war, for the creation or issue of redeemable stock, is deemed to have the same effect as if the Act had been in operation at the time the resolution was passed.
Under the Statutory Companies (Redeemable Stock) Act 1915, what may a statutory company do with sums it sets aside from revenue for the purpose of redeeming redeemable stock?

Section 1(3) provides that a company may set aside out of revenue (after providing for interest on any loan or on any guaranteed, preference or debenture stock and for other fixed charges and obligations) such sums as it considers proper to form a fund for the redemption at maturity of any redeemable preference or debenture stock that is to be redeemed wholly or partly in cash. Those sums, and the income from them, may be invested in securities in which trustees are authorised by law to invest trust funds, or in any other securities (not being securities of the company except as provided) in which the company may be authorised to invest by resolution at a general meeting. The sums must be applied for redemption at maturity, but may also, if the directors think fit, be applied at any time in the purchase of such stock at a price not exceeding the redemption price, with any stock so purchased being cancelled.

What disclosure must a statutory company make to its stockholders under the Statutory Companies (Redeemable Stock) Act 1915, and what criminal penalties does the Act impose for breach of its provisions?

The Act does not prescribe any stockholder disclosure requirements and does not impose any criminal penalties for breach. It is a short enabling statute: section 1 empowers statutory companies to create redeemable preference or debenture stock on terms set out in a special-meeting resolution, section 2 contains definitions and application provisions, and section 3 is the short title. Any disclosure or enforcement duties would have to come from the company's own "special Act" (as defined in section 2(1)), general companies legislation, or the Treasury consent conditions under section 1(5), not from this Act. I cannot supply those details from this Act.
What does section 4(1) of the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company Act 2017 provide are the Company's objects?

Section 4(1) (substituting section 6 of the 1930 Act) provides that the company's objects are to carry on business as a general commercial company. Section 4(2) adds that those objects include maintaining, preserving, managing and improving the fishery.
Under the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company Act 2017, do the Companies Clauses Consolidation Act 1845 or the Companies Clauses Act 1863 still apply to the Company?
Section 5 provides that no provision of the 1845 Act or the 1863 Act shall apply to the Company.
If model articles adopted under section 3 of the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company Act 2017 conflict with the 1930 Act, which provision prevails before section 5 comes into force?

Section 3(3)(a) provides that until the day on which section 5 comes into force in accordance with section 20, the 1930 Act provision prevails. Section 3(3)(b) provides that on and after that day, the adopted model articles prevail.
How does section 2 of the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company Act 2017 define "the 2006 Act" and "the Company"?
Section 2 defines "the 2006 Act" as the Companies Act 2006 and "the Company" as the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company incorporated by section 5 of the 1930 Act.
Who is a "missing shareholder" for Part 3 of the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company Act 2017?

Section 12(1) defines a "missing shareholder" as a shareholder whose name is listed in the register of shareholders kept by the Company and in respect of whom the conditions in subsection (2) are met, and includes a person entitled to the shares by transmission. Section 12(5) explains transmission by heir, successor or assign.
When does Part 3 of the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company Act 2017 come into force?
Section 20(2) provides that Part 3 comes into force on the day after the day on which the Company is first registered under the Companies Act 2006.
What deposit must accompany a claim under section 13(5) of the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company Act 2017, and when is it returned?

Section 13(5) provides that a written notice claiming entitlement must be accompanied by a £50 deposit payable to the Company, which shall be returned to the claimant in the event that the claimant succeeds in establishing the claim.
What is the maximum fine under the Faversham Oyster Fishery Company Act 2017 for selling oysters outside the fishery without permission?

The Act does not prescribe a maximum fine for selling oysters outside the fishery or any similar criminal offence. It modernises the Company's constitution and contains detailed provisions on missing shareholders and share sales, but it does not set out general criminal penalties of that kind. I cannot supply such a figure from this Act.
What collective citation does section 1(2) of the Durham Markets Company Act 1992 authorise?
Section 1(2) provides that the Durham Markets Company Acts 1851 and 1965 and this Act may be cited as the Durham Markets Company Acts 1851 to 1992.
What may the Company do under section 3 of the Durham Markets Company Act 1992 notwithstanding its governing instruments?

Section 3 provides that, notwithstanding anything in any enactment or other instrument constituting or regulating the Company, the Company may exercise the powers of paragraph 6(2) of Schedule 21 to the Companies Act 1985 (effect of registration under section 680 of that Act) and adopt Table A with such modifications as appear to them to be appropriate.
What change does Schedule 1 paragraph 4 make to section XXXIII of the Act of 1851 about tolls for markets?

Paragraph 4 substitutes "such charges as the Company may determine" for the previous caps tied to Schedule (B), and replaces references to stallages, rents and tolls with "charges" in the places specified.
What borrowing ceiling is set for the Company by Schedule 1 paragraph 12 to the Durham Markets Company Act 1992?

Paragraph 12 inserts new subsection (2) of section 8 of the 1965 Act: the Company may borrow or raise or secure the payment of money not exceeding in the whole two million pounds or such larger amount as the Company may from time to time in general meeting determine.
When are the provisions listed in Part II of Schedule 2 to the Durham Markets Company Act 1992 repealed?
Section 5(2) provides that upon the adoption of Table A in accordance with section 3 of this Act the provisions of the Acts of 1851 and 1965 specified in Part II of Schedule 2 are repealed.
What does section 6 of the Durham Markets Company Act 1992 say about who pays the costs of the Act?

Section 6 provides that the costs, charges and expenses preliminary to and of and incidental to the preparing, applying for, obtaining and passing of this Act or otherwise in relation thereto shall be paid by the Company and may in whole or in part be defrayed out of revenue.
What power does new section 7A(3) inserted by Schedule 1 paragraph 10 confer regarding land acquisition?

Section 7A(3) (inserted into the 1965 Act) provides that the Company may acquire other land by agreement, whether by purchase, exchange, lease, licence or otherwise, for the purpose of developing it or of procuring its development together with land already belonging to them.
What is the statutory cap on directors' remuneration under the Durham Markets Company Act 1992?

The Act does not set any cap on directors' remuneration. It amends earlier Durham Markets legislation, permits adoption of Table A, and updates borrowing, land and charging powers. I cannot supply a remuneration cap from this Act.
What is the authorised share capital substituted by Schedule 1 paragraph 1 to the Price's Patent Candle Company Limited Act 1992 for section XIV of the 1857 Act?
Paragraph 1 substitutes a new section XIV providing that the capital of the Company shall be £1,200,000 divided into shares of £16 each.
What overall borrowing limit does new section XIX in the 1857 Act (substituted by Schedule 1 paragraph 2) impose?

New section XIX(1) provides that the amount of money which the Company may borrow or raise or secure for the purposes of or in connection with their undertaking shall not exceed in the whole two million pounds or such larger amount as the Company may from time to time in general meeting determine.
What does section 4 of the Price's Patent Candle Company Limited Act 1992 permit when the Company is registered under the Companies Act 1985?

Section 4 provides that, notwithstanding anything in any enactment or other instrument constituting or regulating the Company, when registered under the Companies Act 1985 as a company limited by shares the Company may exercise the powers of paragraph 6(2) of Schedule 21 to that Act and adopt Table A with such modifications as appear appropriate.
What does section 5(1) of the Price's Patent Candle Company Limited Act 1992 repeal with immediate effect?
Section 5(1) provides that section 17 (Qualification of directors) of Price's Patent Candle Company Limited Act 1920 shall cease to have effect.
When are the Part II provisions of Schedule 2 to the Price's Patent Candle Company Limited Act 1992 repealed?
Section 5(3) provides that upon the adoption of Table A in accordance with section 4 of this Act the provisions of the existing Acts specified in Part II of Schedule 2 are repealed.
Who bears the costs of obtaining the Price's Patent Candle Company Limited Act 1992?

Section 6 provides that the costs, charges and expenses preliminary to and of and incidental to the preparing, applying for, obtaining and passing of this Act or otherwise in relation thereto shall be paid by the Company and may in whole or in part be defrayed out of revenue.
What does paragraph 12A inserted into the Schedule to the 1912 Act by Schedule 1 paragraph 4 authorise?

Paragraph 12A (subject to section XIX of the 1857 Act) authorises the Company to borrow and raise money for the purposes of the undertaking in any manner and secure repayment by mortgage, charge, standard security, lien or other security on the whole or any part of the Company's property or assets (including uncalled capital), and similarly to secure obligations or liabilities.
What annual corporation tax rate on the Company's profits does the Price's Patent Candle Company Limited Act 1992 prescribe?

The Act does not prescribe any corporation tax rate. It amends the Company's capital and borrowing powers, repeals spent provisions, and facilitates adoption of Table A. I cannot supply a tax rate from this Act.
Under section 1(1) of the Company Securities (Insider Dealing) Act 1985, what three conditions must the insider's information satisfy (in addition to being connected with the company)?

Section 1(1) requires information which (a) he holds by virtue of being connected with the company, (b) it would be reasonable to expect a person so connected, in the position by virtue of which he is so connected, not to disclose except for the proper performance of the functions attaching to that position, and (c) he knows is unpublished price sensitive information in relation to those securities.
Does section 3(1)(a) of the Company Securities (Insider Dealing) Act 1985 allow dealing where the individual acts without a view to profit or loss from the information?

Yes. Section 3(1)(a) provides that sections 1 and 2 do not prohibit an individual by reason of his having any information from doing any particular thing otherwise than with a view to the making of a profit or the avoidance of a loss (whether for himself or another person) by the use of that information.
How does section 10 of the Company Securities (Insider Dealing) Act 1985 define "unpublished price sensitive information"?

Section 10 provides that such information is information which (a) relates to specific matters relating or of concern (directly or indirectly) to that company (and is not merely of a general nature), and (b) is not generally known to those accustomed or likely to deal in those securities but would, if generally known, be likely materially to affect the price of those securities.
Who is "connected with a company" for the Company Securities (Insider Dealing) Act 1985?

Section 9 provides that an individual is connected with a company if, but only if— (a) he is a director of that company or a related company; or (b) he occupies a position as an officer (other than a director) or employee of that company or a related company, or a position involving a professional or business relationship which may reasonably be expected to give him access to unpublished price sensitive information which it would be reasonable to expect him not to disclose except for the proper performance of his functions.
What is the maximum sentence on indictment under section 8(1)(a) of the Company Securities (Insider Dealing) Act 1985?
Section 8(1)(a) provides that on conviction on indictment the offender is liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 7 years or a fine, or both.
When did the Company Securities (Insider Dealing) Act 1985 come into force?
Section 18 provides that the Act comes into force on 1st July 1985.
Does the Company Securities (Insider Dealing) Act 1985 extend to Northern Ireland?
Section 17 provides that the Act does not extend to Northern Ireland.
What civil damages can a private shareholder claim under the Company Securities (Insider Dealing) Act 1985 against an insider who traded on unpublished information?

Section 8(3) expressly provides that no transaction is void or voidable by reason only that it was entered into in contravention of section 1, 2, 4 or 5. The Act does not create or quantify any private right of damages for insider dealing. I cannot supply a damages measure from this Act.
What amendment does paragraph 17 of Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act 1981 make to section 20(2) of the Insurance Companies Act 1958?
Paragraph 17 substitutes for the words "the amount required by section 44 of the Insurance Companies Act 1974" the words "£50,000".
What change does paragraph 19 of Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act 1981 make to section 1(3)(b) of the Employers' Liability (Compulsory Insurance) Act 1969?
Paragraph 19 substitutes for the words from "Great Britain" to "1974" the words "the United Kingdom insurance business of a class specified in Schedule 1 or 2 to the Insurance Companies Act 1981".
How does paragraph 23 of Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act 1981 amend the definition of "liability insurance business" in section 87(1) of the Solicitors Act 1974?
Paragraph 23(a) substitutes a definition providing that "liability insurance business" means insurance business within class 13 in Schedule 2 to the Insurance Companies Act 1981.
What change does paragraph 3 of section 25 of Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act 1981 make to section 3(2) of the Policyholders Protection Act 1975?
Paragraph 3 substitutes for the words from "permitted" to "1974" the words "authorised under section 3 or 4 of the Insurance Companies Act 1981".
What does section 38(2) of the Insurance Companies Act 1981 provide about extent?
Section 38(2) provides that the Act extends to Northern Ireland.
What short title does section 38(1) of the Insurance Companies Act 1981 give the Act?
Section 38(1) provides that the Act may be cited as the Insurance Companies Act 1981.
How does paragraph 29 of Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act 1981 amend Article 101(1) of the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981?
Paragraph 29(a) substitutes wording referring to insurance business within group 2 in Part II of Schedule 2 to the Insurance Companies Act 1981, and paragraph 29(b) substitutes "1981" for "1974".
What is the minimum solvency margin in euros that the Insurance Companies Act 1981 requires for UK general insurers?

The Act does not prescribe a minimum solvency margin in euros. Paragraph 17 of Schedule 4 amends a cross-reference in the 1958 Act to substitute a £50,000 figure for a reference to section 44 of the 1974 Act, but the Act does not state any euro-denominated requirement. I cannot supply a euro minimum from this Act.
What does section 5(3) of the Insurance Companies Act 1980 say about territorial extent?
Section 5(3) provides that the Act extends to Northern Ireland.
How may the Insurance Companies Act 1980 be brought into operation?
Section 5(2) provides that the Act shall come into operation on such day as the Secretary of State may by order made by statutory instrument appoint.
What short title does section 5(1) of the Insurance Companies Act 1980 confer?
Section 5(1) provides that the Act may be cited as the Insurance Companies Act 1980.
What does paragraph 2 of Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act 1980 deem about things done under the 1974 Act before the appointed day?

Schedule 4 paragraph 2 provides that anything done under the Insurance Companies Act 1974 or specified instruments shall, in so far as it had effect immediately before the appointed day, have effect on or after that day in relation to Northern Ireland as if that Act extended to Northern Ireland at the time when that thing was done and the thing was done in relation to Northern Ireland as well as Great Britain.
What does paragraph 8 of Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act 1980 provide about changes of appointed actuaries or directors before the appointed day?

Paragraph 8 provides that Articles 19(2) and 65(2) of the Insurance Companies (Northern Ireland) Order 1976 shall continue to have effect after the appointed day in respect of any changes which take place before that day.
What does regulation 8 in Part II of Schedule 2 to the Insurance Companies Act 1980 substitute in the Insurance Companies (Contents of Advertisements) Regulations 1974?
Regulation 8 substitutes for the words from "Great Britain" onwards in regulation 2 of those Regulations the words "the United Kingdom".
What does paragraph 18 of Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act 1980 do to Article 40 of the Industrial Assurance (Northern Ireland) Order 1979?

Paragraph 18 rewrites Article 40 so that, for the words from the beginning to "Order", there shall be substituted the words "Sections 42 and 43 of the Insurance Companies Act 1974", and substitutes "section 72(6)" for "Article 83(5)".
What FCA authorisation threshold in UK GDPR terms does the Insurance Companies Act 1980 establish for insurance intermediaries?

The Act does not refer to the FCA, UK GDPR, or any GDPR-related authorisation threshold. It extends the Insurance Companies Act 1974 to Northern Ireland and makes consequential amendments and transitional savings. I cannot supply a GDPR threshold from this Act.
What short title does section 89(1) of the Insurance Companies Act 1974 give the Act?
Section 89(1) provides that the Act may be cited as the Insurance Companies Act 1974.
Does the Insurance Companies Act 1974 extend to Northern Ireland?
Section 89(3) provides that the Act shall extend to Northern Ireland (subject to the words indicated by ellipsis in the published text).
When does the Insurance Companies Act 1974 come into operation, subject to section 89?
Section 89(2) provides that, subject to section 89 above, the Act shall come into operation at the expiration of the period of one month beginning with the date on which it is passed.
What amendment does Schedule 1 to the Insurance Companies Act 1974 make to section 433(6) of the Companies Act 1948?
Schedule 1 (under section 88) substitutes for the words "the Assurance Companies Act 1909" the words "the Insurance Companies Act 1974".
What amendment does Schedule 1 make to the Employers' Liability (Compulsory Insurance) Act 1969?

Schedule 1 amends section 1(3)(b) of the Employers' Liability (Compulsory Insurance) Act 1969 by substituting for the words "Part II of the Companies Act 1967" the words "Part I of the Insurance Companies Act 1974".
What does section 88(6) of the Insurance Companies Act 1974 say about the effect of its repeals on sections 16(1) and 17(2)(a) of the Interpretation Act 1978?
Section 88(6) provides that the foregoing provisions of section 88 are without prejudice to sections 16(1) and 17(2)(a) of the Interpretation Act 1978 (effect of repeals).
What does Schedule 2 to the Insurance Companies Act 1974 indicate about repeal of the Insurance Companies Act 1958?
Schedule 2 records that the whole of the Insurance Companies Act 1958, so far as unrepealed, is repealed except for specified sections and provisions listed in the Schedule entry.
What annual policy fee does the Insurance Companies Act 1974 charge for each motor policy written in Great Britain?
The Act does not prescribe any per-policy annual fee for motor insurance. It is primarily a consolidation measure with consequential amendments and repeals. I cannot supply such a fee from this Act.
What does section 50(1) of the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973 provide about the Life Assurance Act 1774 and group policies?

Section 50(1) provides that section 2 of the Life Assurance Act 1774 shall not invalidate a policy for the benefit of unnamed persons from time to time falling within a specified class or description if the class or description is stated in the policy with sufficient particularity to make it possible to establish the identity of all persons who at any given time are entitled to benefit under the policy.
Does section 50(2) of the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973 apply only to policies effected after the Act was passed?
No. Section 50(2) provides that section 50 applies to policies effected before the passing of this Act as well as to policies effected thereafter.
What amendment does paragraph 16(a) of Schedule 1 to the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973 make to section 111(1) of the Companies Act 1967?
Paragraph 16(a) inserts after the words "the Protection of Depositors Act 1963" where they first occur the words "or section 20(2) to (4) of the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973".
What does paragraph 17(a) of Schedule 1 to the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973 add to section 116(1) of the Companies Act 1967?
Paragraph 17(a) inserts after the words "of this Act" the words "or any requirements imposed under section 20(2) to (4) of the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973".
What short title does section 57(1) of the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973 confer?
Section 57(1) provides that the Act may be cited as the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973.
What does paragraph 16(c) of Schedule 1 to the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973 substitute in section 111(1)(c) of the Companies Act 1967?
Paragraph 16(c) substitutes for the words "consider whether or not they should exercise with respect to the body" the word "exercise".
What does paragraph 17(b) of Schedule 1 add to section 116(2) of the Companies Act 1967?
Paragraph 17(b) adds at the end the words "or section 20(2) to (4) of the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973".
What limit on actuarial reserves for long-term business does the Insurance Companies Amendment Act 1973 prescribe?

The Act does not set numerical limits on actuarial reserves for long-term business. Section 50 validates certain group policies and Schedule 1 makes minor amendments to the Companies Act 1967. I cannot supply a reserve limit from this Act.
What grants may the Secretary of State make under section 1(5) of the Transport Holding Company Act 1972?

Section 1(5) provides that the Secretary of State may with the approval of the Treasury make to the company grants out of money provided by Parliament towards expenses incurred by the company in disposing of assets or preparing for their dissolution, or towards discharging liabilities imposed on the company under section 2 of this Act.
What does section 1(8) of the Transport Holding Company Act 1972 say "securities" and "subsidiary" mean?
Section 1(8) provides that "securities" and "subsidiary" have the meanings assigned to them by section 92(1) of the Transport Act 1962.
What must the Secretary of State do under section 2(2) of the Transport Holding Company Act 1972 regarding pension rights?

Section 2(2) requires the Secretary of State by order to make provision for safeguarding, in such cases and manner as may be determined by or under the order, the pension rights described in paragraphs (a) and (b) of that subsection.
What does section 2(4) of the Transport Holding Company Act 1972 require the Secretary of State to provide by regulations?

Section 2(4) requires the Secretary of State by regulations to require the payment by the National Freight Corporation, in such cases and to such extent as may be determined by or under the regulations, of compensation to or in respect of any person meeting the conditions in paragraphs (a) and (b).
How are orders and regulations under section 2 of the Transport Holding Company Act 1972 to be made?

Section 2(5) provides that the powers to make orders and regulations conferred by section 2 shall be exercisable by statutory instrument, and any such statutory instrument shall be subject to annulment in pursuance of a resolution of either House of Parliament.
What is a "relevant subsidiary" in section 2(6) of the Transport Holding Company Act 1972?

Section 2(6) defines "relevant subsidiary" as a body which after the passing of this Act ceased or is to cease to be a subsidiary of the company, and defines "transfer date" as the date on which that body ceased or is to cease to be a subsidiary.
Does the Transport Holding Company Act 1972 extend to Northern Ireland?
Section 3(2) provides that the Act extends to Northern Ireland.
What maximum weekly redundancy payment does the Transport Holding Company Act 1972 guarantee to each redundant employee?

The Act does not fix any maximum weekly redundancy payment or schedule of compensation amounts for employees. Section 2 authorises orders and regulations dealing with pensions and compensation in broad terms, with detail left to subordinate legislation. I cannot supply a maximum weekly figure from this Act alone.
What does section 44(2) of the Insurance Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 1968 require when an insurance company to which the Act applies is being wound up?

Section 44(2) provides that the value of a policy of any class or of a liability under such a policy requiring to be valued in the winding up shall be estimated in the manner applicable to policies and liabilities of that class provided by Schedule 3.
What status do the rules in Schedules 3 and 4 have under section 44(3) of the Insurance Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 1968?

Section 44(3) provides that the rules in Schedules 3 and 4 shall be of the same force, and may be revoked or amended, as if they were rules made in pursuance of section 21 of the Interpretation Act (Northern Ireland) 1954.
How is a life policy valued under paragraph 1 of Schedule 3 to the Insurance Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 1968?

Paragraph 1(1) provides that the value of a life policy shall be the difference between the present value of the reversion in the sum assured according to the contingency upon which it is payable (including any bonus or addition made before the commencement of the winding up) and the present value of the future annual premiums.
What rate of interest does paragraph 1(2) of Schedule 3 assume when calculating present values for life policies?
Paragraph 1(2) provides that interest shall be assumed at such rate, and the rate of mortality according to such tables, as the court may direct.
How are annuities valued under paragraph 2 of Schedule 3?

Paragraph 2 provides that an annuity shall be valued according to the tables used by the company which granted the annuity at the time of granting it, or, where those tables cannot be ascertained or adopted to the satisfaction of the court, according to such rate of interest and table of mortality as the court may direct.
What must the liquidator do under Schedule 4 to the Insurance Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 1968 when a company is wound up by or under court supervision?

Schedule 4 provides that the liquidator shall (a) ascertain the value of the liability of the company to each person appearing by the books to be entitled to or interested in policies, and (b) give notice of that value to such persons in such manner as the court may direct; and any person to whom notice is given shall be bound by the value unless he gives notice of intention to dispute in the manner and within a time prescribed by rule or order of the court.
What short title does section 74 confer?
Section 74 provides that the Act may be cited as the Insurance Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 1968.
What limit on policyholder compensation does the Insurance Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 1968 fix for insolvent life offices?

The Act does not state any monetary cap on policyholder compensation in insolvency. It contains winding-up valuation rules in section 44 and Schedules 3 and 4. I cannot supply a compensation cap from this Act.
Who may carry on industrial assurance business in the Isle of Man or Channel Islands under section 60(3) of the Companies Act 1967?

Section 60(3) provides that no person shall carry on in the Isle of Man or any of the Channel Islands industrial assurance business except— (a) a company incorporated (whether under the Companies Act 1948 or otherwise) or a registered society; or (b) such a society as falls within paragraph (b) of the last foregoing subsection.
What maximum fine on summary conviction does section 86(3) of the Companies Act 1967 prescribe for an industrial assurance company guilty of an offence under that section?
Section 86(3) provides that a company guilty of an offence under section 86 shall be liable, on summary conviction, to a fine not exceeding £200.
What maximum fine does section 87 of the Companies Act 1967 prescribe for a friendly society registered under the Friendly Societies Act 1896 for an offence under the Industrial Assurance Act 1923?

Section 87 provides that the maximum penalty shall, instead of being a fine not exceeding £100 (or £50 a day for a continuing offence), be a fine not exceeding £200, and sets out the consequential substitution in section 39(1) of the Industrial Assurance Act 1923.
Who may institute proceedings for an offence under Part II of the Companies Act 1967 in England or Wales?

Section 91 provides that proceedings in respect of an offence under this Part shall not, in England or Wales, be instituted except by, or with the consent of, the Treasury, the Industrial Assurance Commissioner or the Director of Public Prosecutions.
Does Part II of the Companies Act 1967 extend to Northern Ireland?
Section 105 provides that Part II of this Act, and Schedules 5 and 6 thereto, shall not extend to Northern Ireland.
What time limit applies under section 90(2) of the Companies Act 1967 to laying an information for a summary offence triable in England and Wales?

Section 90(2) provides that an information may be tried if it is laid at any time within 3 years after the commission of the offence and within 12 months after the date on which evidence sufficient in the opinion of the DPP, Secretary of State or Industrial Assurance Commissioner to justify the proceedings comes to his knowledge.
When is a director deemed to be a director for section 89(1) of the Companies Act 1967?

Section 89(2) provides that a person shall be deemed to be a director of a body corporate if he is a person in accordance with whose directions or instructions the directors of the body or any of them act.
What annual FSCS levy rate on general insurance premiums does the Companies Act 1967 prescribe?

The Act does not refer to the Financial Services Compensation Scheme or prescribe any levy rate on premiums. Part II concerns industrial assurance offences and related penalties and procedures. I cannot supply an FSCS levy rate from this Act.
How are Northern Ireland winding-up orders enforced in England and Scotland under section 246(1) of the Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 1960?

Section 246(1) provides that any order made by the court in Northern Ireland for or in the course of winding up a company shall be enforced in England and Scotland in the courts that would respectively have jurisdiction if the company were registered in England or Scotland, and in the same manner as if the order had been made by those courts.
What evidence of a Northern Ireland winding-up order suffices for enforcement under section 246(2)?

Section 246(2) provides that an office copy of the order shall be produced to the proper officer of the court required to enforce it, and the production of an office copy shall be sufficient evidence of the order.
Who may act as commissioners for taking evidence under section 312(1) when a company is wound up in Northern Ireland?

Section 312(1) provides that the judges of the county courts in England who sit at places more than twenty miles from the General Post Office, Northern Ireland county court judges and recorders, and the sheriffs of counties in Scotland, shall be commissioners for taking evidence under this Act in those circumstances.
To whom must an order for examination in Scotland be directed under section 313(2)?
Section 313(2) provides that the order or commission shall be directed to the sheriff of the county in which the person to be examined is residing or happens to be for the time.
What happens if a person summoned under section 313(3) fails to appear or refuses to be examined?

Section 313(4) provides that the sheriff shall proceed against him as a witness or haver duly cited and failing to appear or refusing to give evidence or make production may be proceeded against by the law of Scotland.
What fees and allowances are mentioned in section 313(5)?

Section 313(5) provides that the sheriff shall be entitled to such fees, and the witness to such allowances, as sheriffs when acting as commissioners under appointment from the Court of Session and as witnesses and havers are entitled to in the like cases according to the law and practice of Scotland.
What short title does section 405(1) confer?
Section 405(1) provides that the Act may be cited as the Companies Act (Northern Ireland), 1960.
What fee must a company pay to Companies House when filing its annual return under the Companies Act (Northern Ireland) 1960?

The Act does not prescribe any filing fee payable to Companies House (or any registrar) for annual returns. The surviving provisions excerpted concern cross-border enforcement of winding-up orders and Scottish examinations of witnesses. I cannot supply an annual return fee from this Act.
To whom must interest or dividends on deposits under section 19(1) of the Insurance Companies Act 1958 be paid?

Section 19(1) provides that interest or dividends accruing due on a sum deposited with the Accountant General of the Supreme Court under section 2 of the Assurance Companies Act 1909 or paragraph 1 of Schedule 2 to this Act, on securities in which a sum so deposited is invested or on securities so deposited, shall be paid to the company.
What matters may regulations under section 20(1) of the Insurance Companies Act 1958 cover?

Section 20(1) authorises regulations with respect to applications for warrants, payment of deposits, laying out or investment of deposits by the Accountant General, deposit of securities in lieu of money, payment of interest or dividends, and withdrawal and transfer of deposits.
What condition in section 20(2)(a) of the Insurance Companies Act 1958 must be satisfied for a general-business company to withdraw a deposit?

Section 20(2)(a) provides that, in the case of a company carrying on general business (whether with or without long term business), the Industrial Assurance Commissioner must be satisfied that the value of the company's assets exceeds the amount of its liabilities by £50,000.
What alternative tests does section 20(2)(b) set for a long-term-only company wishing to withdraw a deposit?

Section 20(2)(b) provides that for a company carrying on long term business but not general business, either it has a paid up share capital of fifty thousand pounds or more, or the asset/liability tests in sub-paragraphs (i) and (ii) are met as there described.
Who may make regulations under the Insurance Companies Act 1958 under section 34(1)?
Section 34(1) provides that the Treasury may make regulations under this Act for any purpose for which regulations are authorised or required to be made thereunder.
What parliamentary control applies to regulations under section 34(3) of the Insurance Companies Act 1958?
Section 34(3) provides that any statutory instrument containing regulations made under this Act shall be subject to annulment in pursuance of a resolution of either House of Parliament.
Does the Insurance Companies Act 1958 extend to Northern Ireland?
Section 37(3) provides that the Act shall not extend to Northern Ireland.
What Solvency II risk margin percentage does the Insurance Companies Act 1958 require for UK life insurers?

The Act does not refer to Solvency II or prescribe any risk margin percentage. It consolidates earlier assurance-company law with provisions such as deposits (sections 19–20) and regulation-making powers (section 34). I cannot supply a Solvency II percentage from this Act.
What maximum preferential amount does section 91(1) of the Companies Act 1947 prescribe for wages or salary of a clerk or servant in winding up?

Section 91(1) provides that the maximum amount to which priority is to be given shall be two hundred pounds (instead of fifty pounds in the cases in paragraphs (a) and (c) or twenty-five pounds in paragraph (b)).
What priority period for a workman or labourer's wages does section 91(2) of the Companies Act 1947 specify?
Section 91(2) provides that the period within which services must have been rendered for wages to have priority shall be the same as for a clerk or servant—four months (instead of two months).
What debts does section 91(5) of the Companies Act 1947 include in priority payments?

Section 91(5) provides that the priority debts shall include all accrued holiday remuneration becoming payable to a clerk, servant, workman or labourer (or on death to any other person in his right) on termination of employment before or by effect of the winding-up order or resolution.
What personal liability does section 99(1) impose on the Crown or successor in title for a rentcharge when land vests on dissolution?

Section 99(1) provides that where land vests subject to a rentcharge in the Crown or another person on dissolution of a company or on disclaimer under section 267 of the principal Act, that shall not, subject to subsection (2), impose on the Crown or that person or successors in title any personal liability in respect of the rentcharge.
What does "the principal Act" mean in the Companies Act 1947?
Section 122(1) provides that "the principal Act" means the Companies Act 1929.
How may the Companies Act 1947 be brought into force?
Section 123(2) provides that the Act shall come into force on such day as the Board of Trade may by order appoint, and different days may be appointed for different provisions.
What short title does section 123(1) of the Companies Act 1947 confer?
Section 123(1) provides that the Act may be cited as the Companies Act 1947.
What statutory rate of interest does the Companies Act 1947 pay on preferential debts from the liquidation estate?

The Act does not fix any statutory interest rate on preferential debts. Section 91 amends priority amounts and periods for wages and holiday remuneration; it does not prescribe interest. I cannot supply an interest rate from this Act.
